votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the string length checks, character extraction, and ASCII value comparisons defined in the Coq specification.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq definitions and specification, correctly implementing string length checks, character extraction, and ASCII value comparisons.
The Python code correctly implements the helper functions and the specification logic, matching the Coq definitions exactly.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute the same result: for empty strings return false, for single-character strings check if the last character is alphabetic, and for longer strings check if the last character is alphabetic AND the second-to-last is a space. The helper functions (last_ascii, second_last_ascii, is_alpha, is_space) produce equivalent results, and the main spec function returns whether res matches the expected value, just like the Coq Prop states res equals the computed value.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute the same result: for empty strings return false, for single-character strings check if it's alphabetic, for longer strings check if the last character is alphabetic AND the second-to-last is a space. The helper functions (last_ascii, second_last_ascii, is_alpha, is_space) all produce equivalent results on concrete inputs. The spec function correctly compares res to the expected value.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute the same result: for empty strings return false, for single-character strings check if the character is alphabetic, and for longer strings check if the last character is alphabetic AND the second-to-last is a space. The helper functions (last_ascii, second_last_ascii, is_alpha, is_space) behave identically on concrete inputs, and the spec function correctly compares res to the expected value.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: it gets last and second-last characters, checks ASCII letter and space via ord-equivalents, and matches the length-based cases exactly. On ASCII strings, results coincide for all inputs.

Python mirrors Coq logic: last and second-last chars, ASCII alpha and space checks, and returns whether res equals the expected boolean for all ASCII strings. Edge cases (empty, length 1, length >=2) are handled identically.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: it computes last/second-last chars, checks ASCII letter and space (code 32), branches by length, and returns whether res equals the expected boolean, matching the Coq spec on all ASCII inputs.
